Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that filters out cases with more than 6 events from an event log table named event_log. The table has columns case_id, activity, timestamp. We need to count the number of events per case_id, exclude those where the count > 6, and return all events for the remaining case_ids.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: This is a classic filtering based on an aggregate condition. We can use a window function or a subquery with HAVING and a JOIN, or IN clause.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Using window function: SELECT * FROM ( SELECT *, COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY case_id) AS event_count FROM event_log ) WHERE event_count <= 6;

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Using IN clause: SELECT * FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN ( SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6 );

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Both are valid in DuckDB. The window function approach is often quite efficient and concise. Lets provide the window function approach as the primary answer, but mention the HAVING approach as well.

Name: Validation - IND
Text: Wait, if we use SELECT *, the column case_event_count will also be returned. The prompt says returns only the events from cases that have six or fewer events in total. Its usually fine, but to strictly return just the event columns, we can drop the count column.

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually, using IN is very clean and doesnt add extra columns: SELECT * FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN ( SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6 ); Lets present both methods as they are both excellent for DuckDB.
